topic: Someone finds an unsigned birthday card in their mailbox
raw_output:
[story 1]

The mail slot clattered, and there it was—a thick, cream-colored card, stark white against the dull gray of the mailbox interior. My fingers, usually steady, trembled slightly as I pulled it out. It was unsigned. Just a neat, looping script wishing someone a happy birthday. A strange tightness gripped my chest, a sudden, unwelcome pressure behind my ribs. I unfolded it slowly, the paper crackling faintly under my touch. Who was this person? The anonymity felt like a deliberate slight, a tiny, sharp pinprick right where I felt most exposed. I folded it back up immediately, shoving it deep into the bottom of my junk drawer, my jaw tight. The afternoon light seemed too bright, the silence of my apartment suddenly too loud.

[story 2]

I was waiting for the delivery truck, and when I retrieved the mail, my gaze snagged on something unexpected. Tucked between a utility bill and a flyer was a birthday card. It was blank on the front, save for a handwritten message inside that was surprisingly warm, filled with effusive well-wishes. A flush crept up my neck, warming my cheeks instantly. My breath hitched. It felt like someone had peered into a very private corner of my day and decided to leave a small, brightly colored window open. I picked it up, turning it over and over, my thumb tracing the uneven edges of the paper. A wave of unexpected warmth washed over me, quickly followed by a profound sense of being watched. I tucked the card into my wallet, suddenly feeling an overwhelming urge to retreat into the quiet solitude of my room.

[story 3]

It was a Tuesday, and the mundane rhythm of waiting for the postman was suddenly interrupted. A card. Unaddressed. It lay there, an anomaly in the predictable stream of junk mail. I picked it up, and the texture of the cardstock felt strangely significant beneath my fingertips. My stomach clenched, a tight, knotted feeling that made my shoulders hunch up toward my ears. I couldn't bring myself to look at the handwriting, afraid of what I might see, or perhaps, afraid of what the lack of an address implied. I held it close to my face for a long moment, inhaling the faint, dry scent of old paper. When I finally placed it back in the box, the air in the hallway seemed to thicken, heavy with unspoken expectations. I needed distance; I needed to put physical space between me and that small, unexpected gesture.
